🦀 Rust · Fearless Contents Cheatsheet
🦀 A BookBank field guide

Rust: Fearless System Programming

A concept-first tour of the language that refuses to choose between speed and safety — written for the age when you'll often have an agent like Claude Code writing the actual code. Learn the ideas well enough to steer, review, and trust what the machine writes back.

in the voice of Richard Feynman · first-principles, playful, intuition-first

▶ Start reading ⚡ Cheatsheet

Before you turn the page

Every other systems language hands you a bargain and hopes you don't read the fine print: you can go fast, if you promise never to make a mistake. Rust tears up that contract. It gives you bare-metal speed and a compiler that simply refuses to build the classic catastrophes — the use-after-free, the data race, the null that wasn't supposed to be there.

Here's the twist this book is built around: you are increasingly going to direct an AI agent to write Rust rather than type every semicolon yourself. That changes what's worth learning. Syntax you can look up — or let the agent produce. Concepts are what let you say "no, model it this way," catch the design mistake in review, and know when Rust is even the right tool. So we spend our time on the ideas, not the punctuation.

🧭
How to read this

Chapters 1–2 sell the idea and hand you the one mental model everything hangs on. Chapters 3–6 are the concepts that make Rust Rust: types that forbid bad states, errors as values, fearless concurrency, and abstractions that cost nothing. Chapter 7 is the toolbox. Chapter 8 is the payoff you asked for — how to actually build Rust with a coding agent. Chapter 9 tells you when to reach for Rust, and when not to.

cover ·

Table of Contents

  1. Why Rust, Why Now The bargain every other language makes — and the one Rust refuses. the why
  2. The Borrow Checker Is a Pair Programmer Ownership, moves, and borrowing — the single model the whole language grows from. the model
  3. Make Illegal States Unrepresentable Enums, no null, and using the type system as a design conversation. types
  4. Errors Are Values, Not Surprises No exceptions. Failure is a value you must handle — and ? makes it painless. failure
  5. Fearless Concurrency The payoff of ownership: data races become compile errors, not 3 a.m. pages. threads
  6. Zero-Cost Abstractions Traits, generics, iterators — expressive code that compiles down to hand-tuned speed. power
  7. Cargo & the Ecosystem One tool that builds, tests, lints, and docs — the tight feedback loop agents love. tooling
  8. Vibe-Coding Rust with Agents The real workflow: how the compiler becomes the guardrail that keeps an agent honest. the payoff
  9. Where Rust Is the Right Call CLIs, WASM, embedded, high-perf services — and the honest list of where not to. decide
cover ·
🔑
About this book

Researched from the official Rust Book, the Reference, and current release notes, and grounded in real-world adoption: as of the 2025 Kernel Maintainers Summit, Rust is a permanent part of the Linux kernel; it ships in production on Android; and it runs at planetary scale under AWS's Firecracker microVMs. It has topped Stack Overflow's "most-admired language" list for nine straight years. Verified against the current stable toolchain (the 1.9x series) on the 2024 edition.

🤖
Why concept-first, for the agent era

You can ask Claude Code to "write me a Rust CLI that parses this log format," and it will. What you still have to supply is judgment: is the data modeled so bad states can't happen? Is this error being swallowed? Should this even be Rust? This book gives you exactly those instincts — so the human stays the architect and the agent stays the very fast, very literal typist.

Press ⌘R or reopen the book to refresh after edits.
